text: Phenolic compounds, as important secondary metabolites in plants, are reported with a wide array of biological activities, while the potential of phenolic compounds to interact with proteins and, thus, influence their structure and function is becoming increasingly clear. Their binding to proteins at the different organizational levels can be of covalent or non-covalent nature and is based on various non-chemical interactions (hydrophobic, hydrogen and ionic). The review is dealing with possible ways and consequences of protein-phenolic complex formation, and their biological and technological significance. Binding of phenolic compounds to proteins may induce structural changes, including changes in protein conformations and formation of soluble or insoluble protein complexes. The bioavailability, stability and biological activity of polyphenols are highly dependent on their binding with proteins. The techno-functional properties of food proteins can also depend on their potential to be modified by bioactive polyphenols. In this review, possible computer-assisted models that can improve our understanding on the relationship between phenolic compounds and proteins are presented. Molecular docking studies can provide in silico insights into the binding sites on the proteins and competition between various phenolic compounds and proteins may be elucidated by means of molecular dynamics studies. Such approaches allow the virtual screening of the large sets of compounds, i.e., in silico identification of potential protein targets for the activity of certain polyphenols. In combination with experimental studies, they can be highly valuable for understanding of protein-phenolic complexations during food processing and the human digestive system, which can be of high relevance for developing new food additives and ingredients with improved functionalities and also for designing of effective bioactive food supplements (nutraceuticals).
